story:
There are two kinds of people: those who think self-care is a scam, and those who keep a special spoon for bath salts. I’m the second kind. After my mom died and my boyfriend left with the air fryer, bathing became my only religion. I kept to a strict order: bleach the tub in a spiral, rinse three times with my grandmother’s enamel cup, seven spoonfuls of salt, two drops of oil, light a candle, hum my mother’s song. The water softened everything sharp.

One Sunday I ran out of eucalyptus. The only open place was a church thrift shop. On a dusty shelf I found a cobalt bottle labeled EBBING. It smelled like rain and cold pennies, oddly familiar. I bought it for $3.99. That night two drops turned the bath iridescent. When I sank in, the pipes sighed—and then a warm, answering “Mm.” The water pressed close, tender. The enamel cup bumped my elbow like a nudge. I poured it over my shoulders and sighed. The mirror cleared and wrote a message I hadn’t traced: rinse thoroughly.

I used the EBBING again. On the third night the bath whispered my name. “Nia,” it breathed. The mirror fogged and wrote STAY. I should have been afraid, but the water knew where I hurt, kneading tension no chiropractor had found. Soon it began to ask for things: stir, hum, slower. I obeyed.

One evening the mirror spelled OFFER. The water nudged my grandmother’s bar of soap—Sailor’s Measure, the brand she swore by—toward the drain. I hesitated, then dipped it. It dissolved instantly. The candle flared. The bath hummed my mother’s song, wordless and perfect. I cried hard, and the bath held me. When I surfaced, the mirror said CLEAN.

Time blurred. My skin never pruned. Nine o’clock was nonnegotiable; if I skipped a night, the pipes moaned. I told my friend Ellie it was “more like communion.” “Don’t give your bathtub your debit card,” she joked. The bath didn’t want money. It wanted devotion. One night the mirror commanded SUBMERGE. I argued, then obeyed.

Underwater, the tub opened into a white ocean. The porcelain rim became a shore. My grandmother sat there in her housecoat, scooping water with the enamel cup. “You can let it rinse you,” she said, “or it will rinse you anyway.” The third time I went under, something vast moved below—not a creature, but aware. It saw me. The next day my phone created a calendar event: RINSE CYCLE - 9 PM. I deleted it; it returned. My boss emailed, “Hope your religious observance goes well tonight.”

By 8:30 the bath had already begun to run itself. The candle lit. The mirror wrote WE ARE A RITUAL. I submerged. The vast thing touched me. My memories separated: joy, grief, fear, love. The water examined them. I surfaced light. GOOD.

The bath wanted more. If I tried music, it cooled until I stopped. Ellie barged in with ice cream. Water slid from the tub and wrapped her ankles. It lettered STAY OUT across the tile. We made a truce: nightly bath, polite boundaries.

When the EBBING ran dry, the church ladies swore they’d never stocked it. At home the tub was already full. “We’re done,” I told it. The faucet stilled. I felt relief, until my phone lit with a new app: Rinse. The logo was the enamel cup. “Welcome to the Rinse Cycle Community. Invite friends for bonus soaks!” Ellie texted, “Why is my phone dripping?” The app spread. I shut off Wi-Fi. I took one last bath. The mirror fogged: BE CAREFUL. CLEAN YOUR DRAIN.

As the water spiraled away, a clump of my grandmother’s silver hair clung to the drain. I wrapped it in tissue and hid it. Now the bath behaves, mostly. The app won’t uninstall. A spa down the street opened with fern walls and a neon sign: Let Go. Same logo as the app. People line up smiling, towels bright as promises.

At home I bathe with plain salt. My own song. My own breath. Sometimes underwater I glimpse the white shore, my grandmother watching, the horizon humming. The vast thing waits beneath, not threatening, just expectant. It doesn’t say STAY anymore. It says, like the tide remembering me: Go.
